trajectory_summary: The call is a compressed verify run that ends in a soft, unanchored deferral. The rep collected most qualification data efficiently but missed every leverage moment — the scam story, the wife on title, the volunteered callback window — and closed on a callback-after-bill model with no locked time, no spouse coordination, and no appointment. The outcome is a lead in limbo: warm enough to re-engage but structurally fragile because nothing is locked.
outcome_dispute: v1 outcome tag is 'unknown' — this call should be tagged 'deferred_callback_no_appointment.' No appointment was booked. The rep committed to texting Patrick and calling back after bill receipt, but no date, time, or structure was confirmed. Per codex analysis_directives.soft_outcome_distinction, a callback without a locked time is materially different from a booked sit. Lead-type is unknown: if this is a paid lead, the bill is a hard gate and the deferral is procedurally correct but structurally incomplete; if this is an organic lead, the rep could have booked the appointment without the bill and collected it at the closer stage — making the entire deferral unnecessary.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Structured deferral vs soft exit — locking the callback
primary_coaching_focus.quote: That's no problem. So I'm gonna text you. And then whenever you get back, you can text me a picture of that graph, and then I can schedule you. Okay?
primary_coaching_focus.why: The rep had all the ingredients for a locked callback at this moment — Patrick's return date (Friday), his preferred window (10–noon mountain time, volunteered 15 seconds later), his wife's title status, and two contact channels. Instead the rep exited on 'whenever you get back' with nothing anchored. This is the single highest-leverage coaching gap on this call because it is the difference between a lead that re-engages and one that goes cold. The fix is mechanical and teachable: confirm the date, lock the window, include the wife, consolidate the channel.

key_moments.quote: And what really happens and I'm you know, if I could see that guy, I probably would throw him off the cliff.
key_moments.stewart_read: Patrick discloses a genuine financial trauma — a refinancing scammer told him to stop paying his mortgage, then disappeared, leaving him with late fees and a damaged credit score. His language ('throw him off the cliff') signals real anger and distrust. The rep's response at 04:56 ('Yeah. That's okay. So...') acknowledges nothing and pivots immediately back to bill collection. This is a trust gift that was not opened. The strategic read — that the rep was deliberately keeping scope tight — is rejected: there is no later move that picks this up, and the call ends without any rapport built on this moment.

key_moments.quote: That's no problem. So I'm gonna text you. And then whenever you get back, you can text me a picture of that graph, and then I can schedule you. Okay?
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep exits on a soft callback with no locked time, no date confirmation, no mention of the 10–noon mountain time window Patrick would volunteer 15 seconds later, and no spouse coordination. 'Whenever you get back' is not a locked appointment — it is a hope. Per codex analysis_directives.soft_outcome_distinction, callbacks with locked times vs 'we'll touch base' have a major delta on no-show rate. The reactive read holds: the rep accepted the situation and exited without structuring the follow-up.

key_moments.quote: I yeah. You know, if you call me, like, between ten and noon, my time mountain time
key_moments.stewart_read: Patrick volunteers a specific callback window unprompted — this is the strongest buying signal in the call. A customer who is disengaged does not give you their preferred call window. The rep responds 'Okay' and does not anchor it: no 'I'll put Friday between 10 and noon mountain time in my notes,' no confirmation of the date, no loop-back to include the wife. The signal was available and the rep did not capture it.

Patrick confirms his wife is on the title at 02:03. The rep acknowledges and moves on. The wife is never mentioned again — not at the callback setup, not at the close. Per codex protocols.spouse_decision, a co-title-holder must be included in the appointment. The rep collected the fact and did not act on it. The callback at 06:16 is structured around Patrick alone, making the eventual appointment structurally vulnerable if the wife is a co-decision-maker.
